mainText:
**North Korea’s New Friends**

North Korea, the world’s most insular state, has hosted an unusual number of high-profile visitors over the past year. Chinese Premier Li Qiang stopped by the capital of Pyongyang last October; the Vietnamese Communist Party’s General Secretary, To Lam, arrived the same day; Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko visited in March; and the Speaker of the Russian Duma, Vyacheslav Volodin, arrived in April, following up on Vladimir Putin’s visit in 2024.

Earlier this week, Chinese President Xi Jinping made his first visit to the country in seven years, timed to coincide with the 65th anniversary of the 1961 Sino-DPRK mutual defense treaty, still China’s sole military alliance. Orderly crowds of flag-waving North Koreans lined Xi’s motorcade route, and thousands of children with balloons packed a massive stage to await him. North Korean dictator Kim Jong Un and Xi appeared there with their wives, flanked by goose-stepping honor guards and greeted with a 21-gun salute.

At a Monday night banquet, Xi stated both countries were “linked by mountains and rivers and share a common destiny.” Xi’s visit, along with the pomp surrounding it, reflected a North Korea in a far stronger position than it has been in years, with a capital richer than it has been in at least a decade. Where that money came from—and what Kim intends to do with his strengthened hand—will shape the security of the Korean Peninsula and the uneasy triangle among Pyongyang, Moscow, and Beijing for years to come.

Since the end of the Cold War, during which North Korea enjoyed the patronage of both China and the Soviet Union, the “Hermit Kingdom” has struggled to find reliable international partners. A brief period beginning in 1998 saw South Korea send aid and make large-scale investments in joint business ventures, hoping for a diplomatic thaw, but détente fizzled out in the 2010s. China remained North Korea’s only steady trade relationship, accounting for more than 90 percent of the DPRK’s international commerce. Although Beijing has sought to keep Pyongyang in its orbit, the relationship isn’t one of friends or equals.

Following North Korea’s first nuclear weapons test in 2006, China backed U.N. sanctions and even imposed its own penalties after a test in 2017. Kim responded by announcing surprise talks with the U.S. in 2018—the next year, Xi took a trip to Pyongyang. Soon after, COVID and the near-total closure of its border with China gutted North Korea’s already-feeble economy, reducing trade with Beijing by around 80 percent. The crisis forced Kim—whose proclamations are usually delusionally upbeat—to admit that many citizens were struggling to find enough food.

Russia’s war in Ukraine changed Pyongyang’s fortunes. A 2025 analysis published by Britain’s Royal United Services Institute estimated that North Korea manufactured up to 40 percent of the roughly 14 million shells fired by Russian cannons since August 2023. Ukrainian defense officials believe that North Korea is helping to build the Shahed-style drones used in Russia’s bombing campaign against Ukrainian cities, and the country has also supplied more than 100 ballistic missiles to the Russian military.

Beyond weapons, since late 2024, more than 14,000 North Korean soldiers have fought for Russia on the front lines against Ukraine. Around 10,000 North Korean engineers and hundreds of its drone operators are also still present in Russia. This has cost the lives of more North Korean soldiers than any conflict since the Korean War. A BBC count of the names engraved on a new Pyongyang memorial suggests some 2,300 died in Russia’s Kursk region alone. Britain’s Ministry of Defense estimated last June that total casualties, including the wounded, surpassed 6,000.

In turn, South Korean intelligence services estimate that Russia paid up to $13 billion for North Korean troops and material—around half of North Korea’s estimated yearly GDP. Marcus Noland, an economist who studies North Korea at the Peterson Institute for International Economics, noted that Russia and China have largely stopped enforcing U.N. Security Council sanctions passed in 2016 and 2017.

North Korea’s economy remains a black box due to the lack of reliable data and a post-COVID reduction in international observers, but the war trade has clearly benefited Pyongyang, or at least its elite. North Kore
